worth_it_explanation: Naming is the quiet infrastructure of an account. A consistent convention that encodes the dimensions the team reports on makes filtering, bulk edits, and automated rules fast and safe, while an ad-hoc scheme forces manual lookups and invites edits to the wrong campaign. The check is a read of the campaign and ad group names. The fix is a rename and a documented convention, low risk and quick, and it pays back every time someone reports on or edits the account.
intro: Campaign and ad group names are how a practitioner finds, filters, and edits the right part of an account. A consistent convention that encodes the dimensions the team cares about, such as campaign type, region, or funnel stage, lets reports be filtered by name and lets bulk edits and automated rules target the right campaigns. When names are inconsistent or carry no useful structure, the same work becomes slow and error-prone. These checks confirm that names follow one convention and that they encode the dimensions the team reports and acts on.

audit_checks.title: Campaigns and ad groups follow no consistent naming convention
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without a convention, finding and filtering campaigns relies on memory, and a bulk edit or an automated rule that matches on name can hit the wrong campaigns. The cost is slower work and a higher chance of an edit landing where it was not meant to.

educate.base:
A naming convention is a shared pattern for how campaigns and ad groups are named, applied the same way across the account. It might place the campaign type, the region, and the funnel stage in a set order, separated by a consistent delimiter, so every name reads the same way.

When no convention exists, names accumulate in whatever style each person used at the time. One campaign reads 'Brand Search', another reads 'search_competitors_us', and a third carries a date no one remembers the meaning of. Finding the right campaign then relies on memory, filtering a report by name returns an incomplete set because the names do not share a pattern, and a bulk edit or an automated rule that matches on a name fragment can catch campaigns it was never meant to touch.

The campaign and ad group lists show the names side by side. A few signs point to a missing convention:
  • Names mix styles, such as spaces in some and underscores in others
  • The same dimension, like region or match type, appears in different positions or words across names
  • Filtering the campaign list by a name fragment misses campaigns that should match
Adopting one convention and renaming to it makes the account searchable and safe to edit in bulk. You may want to agree the convention with the client and document it before renaming, so the pattern is maintained as new campaigns are built.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store with many product lines, names that encode the product category and campaign type let reports roll up by line and let bulk edits target one category at a time. You may want to confirm the convention reflects how the business groups its catalog so the names match the reporting.

investigate.intro: Review the campaign and ad group names for a consistent, shared pattern.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open the Campaigns view and read the campaign names together.
Check whether they share one pattern, including the same delimiter and the same order of dimensions, or whether styles vary.

Filter the list by a dimension you would expect in the names, for example a region, and note whether the filter returns every campaign it should. A filter that misses campaigns points to inconsistent names.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether campaign and ad group names follow one consistent convention. Names that share a pattern and filter reliably pass. Names that mix styles or place the same dimension inconsistently, so filtering misses campaigns, are a fail recorded with examples of the inconsistency.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether the campaign and ad group names follow one consistent pattern to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s campaigns and ad groups follow no consistent naming convention, so names mix styles and place the same dimensions differently. Finding and filtering campaigns then relies on memory, and a bulk edit or automated rule that matches on a name can reach campaigns it was not meant to. {{voice_recommend}} agreeing one convention with the client, documenting it, and renaming to it, so the account is searchable and safe to edit in bulk.
